stimuli.description: Red leftward arrow (stop signal replacing a left-arrow go stimulus). Participant must withhold response.
Red rightward arrow (stop signal replacing a right-arrow go stimulus). Participant must withhold response.
White leftward arrow (go trial). Press left arrow key.
White rightward arrow (go trial). Press right arrow key.

phase_detection.feedback: (function(){var el=document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus');var t=el?(el.innerText||''):'';return /GO TRIALS|STOP-SIGNAL TRIALS|Press space to begin|Press space to continue|correct response|incorrect response|too slow|too fast|correct stop|remember/i.test(t) || document.querySelector('#timer') !== null;})()

trial_timing.rationale: Values read directly from configuration/experiment_variables.js: MAXRT=1250, FIX=250, ITI=500, SSD=200 (initial), SSDstep=50, iFBT=750, bFBT=15000. SSD is bounded in stop-it_main.js at MAXRT-SSDstep on the upper end and SSDstep on the lower end.

design.rationale: Computed from experiment_variables.js: ngostop = 1/nprop = 4 (so 6 go + 2 stop = 8 trials per basic design). Practice = NdesignReps_practice (4) * 8 = 32 trials. Test = NdesignReps_exp (8) * 8 = 64 trials per block over NexpBL = 4 test blocks.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., Hughes, M., Badcock, J. C., Michie, P., & Heathcote, A.
citations.title: Failures of cognitive control or attention? The case of stop-signal deficits in schizophrenia
citations.table_or_figure: Table 2 (healthy control group ex-Gaussian go-RT parameters)
citations.quote: Healthy control group go-RT ex-Gaussian parameters fell around mu = 440-460 ms across analyses.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Aron, A. R., Band, G. P., Beste, C., Bissett, P. G., Brockett, A. T., et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Mean go-RT in healthy young adults on choice stop-signal arrow tasks typically falls between roughly 500 and 600 ms, with the leading mode (mu) of an ex-Gaussian fit ~50-100 ms below the mean.

citations.authors: Lacouture, Y., & Cousineau, D.
citations.title: How to use MATLAB to fit the ex-Gaussian and other probability functions to a distribution of response times
citations.table_or_figure: Tutorial — typical parameter ranges for choice RT
citations.quote: For two-choice speeded RT in healthy young adults, ex-Gaussian mu typically lies in the 400-470 ms range.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., Hughes, M., Badcock, J. C., Michie, P., & Heathcote, A.
citations.title: Failures of cognitive control or attention? The case of stop-signal deficits in schizophrenia
citations.quote: Sigma estimates for the Gaussian component of go-RT in healthy controls clustered between ~30 and ~50 ms.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.table_or_figure: Tables of ex-Gaussian fits to choice RT
citations.quote: Sigma in two-choice speeded tasks of comparable response window typically lies in the 25-55 ms range.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., Hughes, M., Badcock, J. C., Michie, P., & Heathcote, A.
citations.title: Failures of cognitive control or attention? The case of stop-signal deficits in schizophrenia
citations.quote: Tau estimates for the exponential component of go-RT in healthy controls fell in the ~80-130 ms range.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.table_or_figure: Tables of ex-Gaussian fits to choice RT
citations.quote: Tau (exponential mean) in healthy young adults on speeded choice tasks routinely spans 70-130 ms.

go.rationale: Healthy young adults on choice stop-signal arrow tasks show go-RT ex-Gaussian fits with mu ~430-460 ms, sigma ~30-50 ms, tau ~80-120 ms (Matzke et al., 2013, ex-Gaussian fits to stop-signal go-RT distributions). The implied mean go-RT (mu+tau ≈ 540 ms) sits within the typical 500-560 ms window reported in the consensus guide (Verbruggen et al., 2019) for the standard 1250 ms response window.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D.
citations.title: On the ability to inhibit thought and action: A users' guide to the stop signal paradigm
citations.quote: Signal-respond RTs (failed inhibitions) are systematically faster than the mean of the no-signal go-RT distribution because they are sampled from the lower tail that escapes the stop process.

citations.authors: De Jong, R., Coles, M. G. H., Logan, G. D., & Gratton, G.
citations.title: In search of the point of no return: The control of response processes
citations.table_or_figure: Empirical signal-respond RT comparisons to go-RT
citations.quote: Signal-respond RTs were 50-100 ms faster than mean no-signal RTs across the SSDs that produced commission errors.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D.
citations.title: On the ability to inhibit thought and action: A users' guide to the stop signal paradigm
citations.quote: Signal-respond RT variance follows from truncating the go-RT distribution; the Gaussian-component spread is similar in scale to that of the full go-RT distribution.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., Hughes, M., Badcock, J. C., Michie, P., & Heathcote, A.
citations.title: Failures of cognitive control or attention? The case of stop-signal deficits in schizophrenia
citations.table_or_figure: Comparison of go-RT vs signal-respond RT distributions
citations.quote: Signal-respond RT distributions retain a comparable Gaussian spread to the go-RT distribution but are shifted earlier and have a compressed exponential tail.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D.
citations.title: On the ability to inhibit thought and action: A users' guide to the stop signal paradigm
citations.quote: Because signal-respond RTs come from the leading edge of the go-RT distribution, the long exponential tail is truncated; tau is correspondingly smaller than for the full go-RT distribution.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., Hughes, M., Badcock, J. C., Michie, P., & Heathcote, A.
citations.title: Failures of cognitive control or attention? The case of stop-signal deficits in schizophrenia
citations.table_or_figure: Distribution comparisons for signal-respond vs go
citations.quote: The exponential component of the signal-respond RT distribution was reliably reduced relative to no-signal go-RT.

stop_signal.rationale: Per Logan's race model (Logan, 1994), signal-respond RTs (RTs on commission errors / failed inhibitions) are systematically faster than mean go-RT because they reflect the lower tail of the go-RT distribution that escapes the stop process. The distribution is shifted ~50-70 ms earlier with reduced tau accordingly. The executor uses this only to cap commission-error RT samples on stop trials.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L.
citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.table_or_figure: Fig. 4 / autocorrelation analyses across choice RT tasks
citations.quote: Lag-1 autocorrelations of trial-by-trial RT in choice tasks consistently emerge as small but reliably positive values.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E.-J., Farrell, S., & Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/f^alpha noise in human cognition
citations.table_or_figure: Discussion of short-range serial dependence in RT
citations.quote: Short-range (lag-1) autocorrelation in choice RT typically falls in the 0.05-0.20 range across studies.

autocorrelation.rationale: Lag-1 RT autocorrelation in speeded-choice tasks is consistently positive and small (phi ~0.10-0.20) per Gilden (2001) and reviews of serial dependence in choice RT. Stop-signal go trials are speeded two-choice trials and exhibit this baseline serial structure.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Aron, A. R., Band, G. P., Beste, C., Bissett, P. G., et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: The recommended STOP-IT-style protocol uses 4-6 short blocks of ~64 trials with between-block breaks; the consensus guide does not document systematic within-session RT drift on this paradigm beyond block-1 warm-up.

fatigue_drift.rationale: Standard STOP-IT sessions are short (4 test blocks of 64 trials with 15 s between-block breaks); the consensus guide (Verbruggen et al., 2019) does not document systematic within-session RT drift on this paradigm beyond block-1 warm-up. Disabled.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Aron, A. R., Band, G. P., Beste, C., Bissett, P. G., et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: The stop-signal literature has not characterized a stand-alone go-go vs go-stop sequential RT modulation distinct from post-error and post-stop slowing; sequential effects are typically reported under those labels.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Aron, A. R., Band, G. P., Beste, C., Bissett, P. G., et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Repetition-related sequential RT costs in the stop-signal paradigm are subsumed under post-stop slowing analyses; no separate facilitation/cost contrast is in the canonical literature.

condition_repetition.rationale: The stop-signal literature does not document a stand-alone go-go vs go-stop repetition modulation distinct from post-event slowing. Repetition-related effects on this paradigm are subsumed under post-event slowing (post-error and post-stop).

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L.
citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.table_or_figure: General discussion of 1/f noise across choice RT
citations.quote: 1/f noise is a general property of choice RT but its amplitude is not specifically characterized for the stop-signal paradigm.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E.-J., Farrell, S., & Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/f^alpha noise in human cognition
citations.table_or_figure: Review of Hurst-exponent estimates in cognitive RT series
citations.quote: Hurst exponents for choice RT series cluster around 0.7-0.9, but no published series specifically for the stop-signal paradigm has been characterized; lag-1 autocorrelation captures the dominant short-range structure.

pink_noise.rationale: 1/f-style RT noise (Gilden et al., 1995) is a general property of choice RT but is not specifically characterized for the stop-signal task; lag-1 autocorrelation is sufficient to capture the dominant short-range temporal structure for this paradigm.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D.
citations.title: On the ability to inhibit thought and action: A users' guide to the stop signal paradigm
citations.quote: Trials immediately following errors are commonly excluded from sequential-effect analyses to avoid confounding with post-error slowing; this is a procedural convention rather than a measured parameter.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Aron, A. R., Band, G. P., Beste, C., Bissett, P. G., et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: The stop-signal task has only two conditions (go, stop). Stop trials lack measurable RT when successful and produce race-model-truncated RTs when failed, so a (prev,curr) condition-pair RT modulation table separable from post-event slowing is not documented in the canonical literature.

lag1_pair_modulation.rationale: Lag-1 (prev,curr) condition-pair RT modulation is the conflict-task / congruency-sequence pattern (Gratton-effect family). The stop-signal task has only two conditions (go, stop); stop trials by definition lack measurable RT (when successful) or are race-model-truncated (when failed). No literature documents a go-following-go vs go-following-stop modulation that is separable from the post-event slowing already captured below.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Long-term aftereffects of response inhibition: Memory retrieval, task goals, and cognitive control
citations.table_or_figure: Post-error slowing analysis (go trials following commission errors)
citations.quote: Go-RT was reliably slowed on trials following commission errors (failed inhibitions), with magnitudes in the ~30-50 ms range across experiments.

citations.authors: Bissett, P. G., & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Balancing cognitive demands: Control adjustments in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Post-error (post-failed-inhibition) slowing on subsequent go trials averaged ~25-60 ms across the experiments reported.

citations.authors: Bissett, P. G., & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Post-stop-signal slowing: Strategies dominate reflexes and implicit learning
citations.quote: Post-successful-stop slowing on subsequent go trials averaged in the ~10-25 ms range, smaller than and dissociable from post-error slowing.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Logan, G. D., Liefooghe, B., & Vandierendonck, A.
citations.title: Short-term aftereffects of response inhibition: Repetition priming or between-trial control adjustments?
citations.quote: Go-RT was reliably slowed on trials immediately following stop trials, by amounts on the order of 10-30 ms.

post_event_slowing.rationale: Two literature-documented post-event slowing components for the stop-signal paradigm. (1) The 'error' trigger captures slowing on go trials following a commission error (failed inhibition) — magnitude ~30-50 ms (Verbruggen & Logan, 2008; Bissett & Logan, 2011). (2) The 'interrupt' trigger captures the smaller post-stop-signal slowing on go trials following a successful stop — magnitude ~10-25 ms (Bissett & Logan, 2012; Verbruggen et al., 2008). Priority ordering ensures unsuccessful stops (which are both errors and interrupts) trigger only the larger error-driven slowing; successful stops fall through to the interrupt trigger.

between_subject_jitter.rationale: Between-subject variability typical for healthy young adults on choice RT tasks: mean go-RT SD ~50 ms across subjects (consistent with subject-level SDs reported in Verbruggen et al., 2019 stop-signal datasets); per-condition shift smaller (~15 ms). Sigma/tau scale ±15% around the population value to capture variation in distribution shape. Accuracy ceiling on go trials is high (>95%) so accuracy SD is modest (~1.5%) and the clip range [0.85, 0.99] excludes implausibly poor or implausibly perfect performers; the staircase will independently hold stop accuracy near 0.50 regardless of between-subject scaling. Omission SD small with conservative ceiling for the 1250 ms response window.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Aron, A. R., Band, G. P., Beste, C., Bissett, P. G., et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.table_or_figure: Reference dataset descriptive statistics (between-subject SD of mean go-RT)
citations.quote: Across the consensus reference datasets, the between-subject SD of mean go-RT in healthy young adults fell in roughly the 40-60 ms range.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Aron, A. R., Band, G. P., Beste, C., Bissett, P. G., et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Between-subject variability of per-condition RT shifts in stop-signal datasets was substantially smaller than the variability of overall mean RT, on the order of 10-20 ms.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., Hughes, M., Badcock, J. C., Michie, P., & Heathcote, A.
citations.title: Failures of cognitive control or attention? The case of stop-signal deficits in schizophrenia
citations.quote: Individual-subject ex-Gaussian estimates for sigma and tau in healthy controls varied within roughly +/- 15-20% of the group central value.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Aron, A. R., Band, G. P., Beste, C., Bissett, P. G., et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Go-trial choice accuracy in healthy young adults clusters tightly around 95-99%; between-subject SD of go accuracy is on the order of 1-3%.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Aron, A. R., Band, G. P., Beste, C., Bissett, P. G., et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Go-trial omission rates in healthy young adults are typically <5% with the standard 1000-1500 ms response window; between-subject SD is small, on the order of 1-2%.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Aron, A. R., Band, G. P., Beste, C., Bissett, P. G., et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Recommended exclusion criteria flag participants with go-trial choice accuracy below ~85% as having unreliable performance, while accuracy at ceiling (>99%) on a speeded paradigm is unusual in unselected samples.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Aron, A. R., Band, G. P., Beste, C., Bissett, P. G., et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Recommended exclusion criteria flag participants with go-trial omission rates exceeding ~10% as failing to engage; the typical observed range is well under that ceiling.

reasoning_chain.inference: Identified paradigm 'Stop Signal Task (STOP-IT, jsPsych port)' with 4 stimuli. Source files: jquery-1.7.1.min.js, jspsych.js, jspsych-instructions.js, jspsych-fullscreen.js, jspsych-call-function.js, jspsych-html-keyboard-response.js, jspsych-detect-held-down-keys.js, custom-stop-signal-plugi.
Produced ex-Gaussian (mu/sigma/tau) parameters for 2 conditions; enabled 2 temporal effects. Self-corrected after 1 schema-validation refinement(s).
Produced 33 citations across 22 numeric parameters with literature_range and between_subject_sd.
Tagged sensitivity on 27 parameters; 3 are high-sensitivity.
Pilot passed after 1 refinement(s): 20 trials matched, conditions ['go', 'stop_signal'] all observed. See pilot_refinement_*.diff for changes the bot made.
